era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Crusader States are in a period of relative stability but facing increasing pressure from Saladin's rise in Egypt and Syria. In Europe, Henry II of England is consolidating his Angevin Empire. The Byzantine Empire under Manuel I is at a high point of military and cultural power. In Asia, the Jin Dynasty rules northern China while the Southern Song Dynasty controls the south. Islamic civilization is fragmented but increasingly unified under strong military leaders.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited specific documentation for 1164)

events.title: Amalric I of Jerusalem becomes King of Jerusalem
events.description: Amalric I becomes King of Jerusalem following the death of his brother Baldwin III. Amalric is an aggressive military leader who pursues expansionist policies, particularly in Egypt. His reign marks a period of Crusader military activity and attempted expansion, though ultimately unsuccessful in the face of Saladin's rising power.
events.certainty_note: Amalric I's accession to the throne is well-documented. However, Baldwin III died in 1163, so Amalric's accession would have occurred in 1163, not 1164. This entry contains a dating error.

events.title: Battle of Harim: Crusaders defeat Nur ad-Din's forces
events.description: The Crusaders, led by Amalric I and Bohemond III of Antioch, defeat Nur ad-Din's forces at the Battle of Harim in northern Syria. This is a significant Crusader victory that temporarily halts Nur ad-Din's expansion and demonstrates the military capability of the Crusader States when unified. However, the victory is not followed up effectively, and Nur ad-Din recovers.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Harim is well-documented in both Crusader and Islamic sources. The date of 1164 is confirmed.

events.title: Henry II of England and Thomas Becket conflict intensifies
events.description: The conflict between King Henry II and Archbishop Thomas Becket of Canterbury escalates. Becket, who had been Henry's chancellor and friend, now opposes the king's attempts to subordinate the Church to royal authority. The dispute centers on the rights and privileges of the Church versus the crown, a fundamental issue in medieval politics. The conflict will culminate in Becket's murder in 1170.

sources.name: Life of Thomas Becket (various contemporary accounts)

events.certainty_note: The Henry II-Thomas Becket conflict is extensively documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Council of Clarendon: Henry II asserts royal authority over the Church
events.description: King Henry II convenes the Council of Clarendon, at which he issues the Constitutio Clarendonis (Constitutions of Clarendon). These constitutions assert royal authority over ecclesiastical appointments and limit the Church's independence from the crown. Thomas Becket initially accepts the constitutions but later repudiates them, leading to his exile and the intensification of the conflict with Henry II.
events.certainty_note: The Council of Clarendon and the Constitutio Clarendonis are well-documented in contemporary sources and the text of the constitutions survives.

disconfirming_evidence: The entry for Amalric I's accession (1164-001) contains a dating error: Baldwin III died in 1163, so Amalric I would have become king in 1163, not 1164. This is a significant correction that affects the chronology of the Crusader States. The Battle of Harim is well-documented and the date of 1164 is correct, but some sources place it in 1163; modern scholarship generally accepts 1164 as the correct date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1164 is dominated by European sources, particularly English and Crusader chronicles. The Henry II-Thomas Becket conflict is extensively documented because it was a major political and ecclesiastical event in medieval Europe. Islamic sources for the Battle of Harim exist but are less detailed than European accounts. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are less well-documented. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and American histories remain absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Harim was a temporary Crusader success, but Nur ad-Din recovered and defeated the Crusaders at Marj al-Rahit in 1166
The Henry II-Thomas Becket conflict escalated from 1164 and culminated in Becket's murder in 1170
The Council of Clarendon was a key event in the broader Henry II-Thomas Becket conflict
